description:
# One pattern compiler. Three call sites. Cascade dies.

## Reframe (2026-05-17, post-.C land)

Earlier framing had an "eligibility gate + cascade fallback" for safety.
Wrong frame. Existing CLIF isn't load-bearing — it's just what we
happen to emit. The arc's value is clearly-expressed, efficient IR,
not preserving artifacts.

Pattern matching is ONE concept. Today it's 5+ specializations
(lower_pattern_bind, lower_multi_clause cascade, lower_case cascade,
lower_with else cascade, standalone Pattern::Tuple TypeTest). ONE
matrix-based pattern compiler replaces all of them. Single-pattern is
the degenerate (1-row) case of the same algorithm.

## The DAG

    A+B (landed)            C (landed — pattern_matrix module)
       └────────────┬──────────┘
                    ▼
            D.0  drop body_might_cps_split fast path
                 (always cont-fn-wrap; inline_tail_calls_once merges)
                    │
                    ▼
            D.1  lower_multi_clause body REPLACED by matrix dispatch
                 (no eligibility gate; matrix is the ONLY path)
                    │
            ┌───────┴───────┐
            ▼               ▼
           F               G
       lower_case      lower_with's else
       (mechanical)    (mechanical)
                    │
                    ▼
           H  lower_pattern_bind → 1-row matrix wrapper
              (extract bitstring/map helpers first, then collapse)
                    │
                    ▼
           45  exhaustiveness + dead-clause warnings

## Removal catalog (by arc completion)

- ~200 LOC: lower_pattern_bind body
- ~150 LOC: three cascade implementations
- ~50 LOC: body_might_cps_split + its fast-path branch
- standalone Pattern::Tuple TypeTest (line 1955)
- next:BlockId threading through pattern recursion
- "matrix-eligible vs cascade-fallback" decision logic (never added)
- Two control-flow models for clause bodies → one
- Two dispatch implementations → one

## What stays

- All IR primitives (TypeTest, TupleField, ListIsNil/Head/Tail,
  MapGet, BitReader*, If)
- cont_fn machinery (mint_cont_fn, switch_to_cont_fn, finalize_arm)
- inline_tail_calls_once (slightly more important work)
- pattern_matrix module (.C) + algorithm
- Typer narrowing + per-spec fold from A+B

## How this makes OTHER problems tractable

- Exhaustiveness / dead-clause warnings (.45): the Decision tree IS
  the data structure for these.
- Future Or-patterns, pinned patterns: one place to extend.
- Bitstring optimization: one entry to attack.
- Eventual case/cond/with unification: matrix is the lingua franca.
- Typer narrowing compounds harder: shared dispatch's TypeTest
  narrows env for ALL downstream leaves (today's per-clause cascade
  narrows only the clause's own arm).
- Per-spec fold (.B) does more work: fewer redundant tests pre-fold
  means more dead-arm collapse per spec.
- Three-path parity simpler: one pattern model in interp/JIT/AOT.

## Sub-tickets

- fz-ul4.43.D.0 — drop body_might_cps_split fast path
- fz-ul4.43.D.1 — matrix dispatch in lower_multi_clause (no gate)
- fz-ul4.43.F — matrix in lower_case
- fz-ul4.43.G — matrix in lower_with's else
- fz-ul4.43.H — lower_pattern_bind = 1-row matrix wrapper
- fz-ul4.45 — exhaustiveness warnings (follow-on)

## Deferred

- .43.E.1 / .43.E.2 — Term::Switch + br_table. For typical dispatch
  widths in fz fixtures (3-5 cases), br_table not net-positive. The
  structural sharing in matrix is the win. Re-file when we have a
  fixture with ≥8-way dense dispatch.

comments.text: fz-ul4.39 verdict (see fz-i82 comment): per-callsite specialization is NOT the cause of fz-i82. Decision-tree dispatch will NOT subsume it. The 'Bonus expected outcome' section in this ticket should be considered withdrawn. Scope here is now just: collapse cross-clause TypeTest duplicates and produce tighter CLIF. fz-i82 stays as its own arc.

Session handoff — design refined after .40-.44 + .37 landed.

CONTEXT: PR landed substantial wins (.40 reachability prune, .42 deletes skip_payoff_free_spec, .44 honest mode flag, .37 multi-print). This ticket is the remaining cherry — the lowering refactor itself.

CURRENT STATE OF lower_multi_clause (src/ir_lower.rs ~1030-1134):
- Per-clause try_blocks cascade
- Each clause runs lower_pattern_bind independently, emitting its own TypeTest
- Fall-through to next try_block on mismatch

REFINED V1 SCOPE (most conservative):
- Only fire when every clause's first param is Pattern::Tuple
- Group by arity; emit one TypeTest per arity group
- Within an arity group, fan out to per-clause atom-at-field-0 dispatch
- All other shapes (List, Atom, Int, Map, As, guards) → fall back to current cascade
- ast_eval qualifies; multi_clause/fib_tailrec/classify_two_clause hit fallback (goldens unchanged)

WHY v1 IS NARROW: Pattern enum has 13 variants. Doing all of them in one ticket is the original sin we're trying to avoid. Tuple-only v1 proves the structural refactor; subsequent tickets enable other discriminators.

REVISED ATOMIC STEPS:
1. Add fn first_param_tuple_arity(clause) -> Option<usize> 
2. Pre-pass: if ALL clauses return Some, partition by arity, preserving original order within partitions
3. Emit one TypeTest per arity at function entry; on success enter shared block; on failure chain to next arity test (or fail)
4. Within shared arity block, emit per-clause sub-cascade: atom check at field 0 (if Pattern::Atom), or just fall through to body (if Pattern::Var)
5. Verify dispatch ORDER preserved (clause N still beats clause N+1 — partition preserves source order within each arity group; between arity groups we must check arities in original-clause order to avoid reordering observable behavior on overlapping patterns — but tuples of different arities can't overlap, so any order between groups is safe)
6. Re-bless ast_eval golden; verify all other fixtures byte-identical
7. Acceptance test: count TypeTest sites in ast_eval CLIF == #arity-groups (2), not #clauses (3)

ALTERNATIVE CONSIDERED & REJECTED: post-lowering peephole pass (path-sensitive constant fold that detects 'block B is on false-branch of block A's same-TypeTest, so block B's TypeTest folds to false'). Cleaner in some ways (no lowering refactor) but requires building dominator + path-condition tracking infrastructure we don't have. Refactoring the lowering is more direct given our current toolset.

OPEN QUESTIONS FOR NEXT SESSION:
- Should arity-group ordering follow source-clause order (safer) or arity-ascending (cosmetic)? Recommend source order.
- For the v1 fallback predicate, do we require Pattern::Tuple at top level, or unwrap Pattern::As(_, inner) too? Recommend top-level-only for v1.
- Guard handling: a guard on a clause means after pattern-bind succeeds we may still fall through to next clause's test. With shared dispatch, 'next clause' must point into the same arity group's next leaf — verify this composes.

ARC REFINED after .43.C land + deeper research:

- .43.A+B landed bundled (-1906 lines CLIF; net spec dedup)
- .43.C landed (pattern_matrix types + compile() unit tests)
- .43.E.1 + .43.E.2 DEFERRED: br_table not net-positive for fz's typical
  dispatch widths. Re-file if/when we have ≥8-way dense dispatch in a
  real fixture. The structural win lives in matrix sharing, not the IR
  primitive.
- .43.D refined: eligibility gate (≥2 clauses share a constructor) +
  cascade fallback for ineligible fns. Protects existing goldens.
- .43.F / .43.G follow D's shape: case clauses, then with's else cascade
- .43.H lands last: lower_pattern_bind becomes 1-row matrix wrapper

Remaining arc: D → F → G → H → 45 (exhaustiveness).

title: fz-ul4.43: one pattern compiler (matrix everywhere)
